That the following be the Fares within the
Borough by set down : —

For a drive from any stand within the
Borough direct to any other stand or
place within the same, without any
delay, for not more than one person
and not exczeding 421b. luggage, 6.

For more than one and not exceeding four
persons with 84lb. luggage, is.

9 o’clock in the morning double fare.

every houror part ofan hourafterwards.

The affairs of the Hospital are managed by
a Committee of 13 members, called in the
Act of Parliament *‘ The Committee of
Management.” This Committee is elected
by the Governors from among their own
number, at their Annual General Meet-
ing, held on the second Monday in
January in each year. No one but a
Governor is eligible to be elected on this.
Committee—r1th George IV., chap. 72.

ADMISSION OF PATIENTS.

Govenors or Subscribers wishing to have
a patient admitted to the house should give
him or her, as the case may be, the
necessary order of admission to the Resident
Medical Officer, Barrington’s Hospital, and.
if there is a bed vacant, and the case is a
suitable one for treatment, the patient will
be received.

Orders of admission must be made on the
printed form, which may be had onapplica-
tion to the Registrar.

Urgent cases are taken in at all hours of
the day or night, without an order of
admission,

The Dispensary is open all day and
night for the treatment of accidents, :

NoTE—As the funds at the disposal of the-
Governors are not large, and as the Institu-
tion was founded principally for the
treatment of accident cases,it is to be hoped
that the subscribers will recommend as
few Medical cases as they possibly can.

In addition to the above, where property

' liable to Estate Duty is settled by the will

of the deceased, or having been settled by
gsome other disposition, passes under that
disposition, on the death of the deceased,
to some other person not competent to dis-
pose thereof, a further duty—called Settle-

from that payment the ad.val. Stamp Duty
(if any) charged on the settlement may be

Small Estates—(1) If the net value of the
Ercperty. real and personal, passing on the
jeceased’s death (exclusive of property
gettled by any prior disposition) does not
exceed £1,000, such property is exempt from
Settlement Estate Duty, and from Legacy
or Succession Duties. (2) And where the
gross value of the unsettled property pass-
ing on the deceased’s death does not ex-
ceed £300 or £500 respectively, and a grant
of probate or administration is taken oui
in a certain special manner, a fixed Duty
of 30s or 50s is payable, and such estate 18
exempt from all other Death Duties.

be paid, if desired, by eight yearly or six-
teen half-yearly instalments

STATUTES OF LIMITATIONS.

Debt.—The limitation, or lapse of time
which bars an action for_a debt or cove-
nant, if founded upon a deed, is 20 years,
but if founded on any less formal agree-
ment, the limitation is six years.

Land.—The limitation to recover land is
12 years from the time the right first
accrued. This time also applies to the re-
covery of land by a mortgagee, or omne
who seeks to recover money chargeable
upon-land. If a right to land accrues to a
person, who is under such disability as
infancy, lunacy, &c., the action may be
brought within six years of the ending of
guch disability or of the disabled person’s
death; but in no case of such disability
must the lapse of time be more than 30
years. .

Rent.—No action to recover arrears of
rent may be brought, except such arrears
have accrued within 6 years of the bring-
ing of the action.

Formal Acknowledgment of liability will.

render of no effect any lapse of time which
has occurred, but a fresh limitation com-
mences from the time of "  such acknow-
ledgment. :

SHOPS ACT, 19i2.

Applies in Ireland onl
cipal boroughs, urban districts, and towns
which have Town (ommisgioners. Bhop-
keepers in the smaller country towns and
villages and in rural districts are exempt.
_premises where any retail trade or
business is carried on are regarded as
shops. Wholesale premises, as such, and
places subject to the Factory and Work-
shops Acts, do not fall under the Shops
Aect. The business of receiving orders for
the manufacture of articles and the sale
and delivery of articles when made is
retail tr'a&e within the meaning of the Act.
Barbers’ and hairdressers’ premises, public
houses, refreshment rooms, and auction
roome (except where goods are sold by
auction wholesale) are shops. Laundry re.
oeiving houses, servants’ registry offices,
and places where articles ave let for hire

to shope in Muni-

are net shops. Stalls in public markets
(with the exception of wholesale markets),
stalls at railway stations for sale of books
are reﬁarded as shops. Fairs and bazaars
and the sale of programmes and cata-
logues at theatres are excluded from the
Shops Act.

All persons,'male and female, are shop
assistants who are wholly or mainly em-
ployed in a shop in connection with the
gerving of customers and receipt of orders
or the despatch of goods. The clerks in
the counting house, who are mainly em-
ployed in keeping the employer's books.
errand boys, carmen and others engaged
in delivering goods at the houses of custo-
mers, or in going round for orders, are not
shop assistants.

This is divided into_the following classes
—Letters, Postcards, Printed Papere, Com-
mercial Papers, Ssamples, and Parcels.

Lettera to British Possessions generally,
to Egypt, the U.8. America, British Postal
Agencies in Morocco, and to H.M. Bhips of
War serving abroad, 1d. per oz.; to all
other places, 24d. for the first oz. and 1id.
for. each additional oz. Printed Papers,
Commercial Papers, and Samples, id. per
9 oz. Minimum for Commercial Papers,
’1d., for Samples 1d. Post Cards, 1d., reply

Telegraphs.

The charge is 6d for the first twelve
words, including the address, and id. for
every additional word. Counting—compound
words, proper names, figures, abbrevia-
tions, &c. : :

The cost of registering an abbreviated
address, which must congist of two words,
ia £1 1s. ;

Telegraph offices are usually open on
week-days from 8 a.m. to 8 p.m. but in
London and most of the largest provincial
towns, offices are always open, day and
night, and in many towns telegrams may
be despatched at any hour from the rail-
way station. ;

Telegrameg in plain language in &any
modern European tongue are charged for
according to the number of words; but all
words not forming part of any modern
European language, and all unintelligible
combinations of letters are charged for at
the rate of five letters to a word.

Exceptions—Words' which are ordinarily
written as one, or coupled by hyphens, as
“mother-in-law,” “forty-seven,” “warehouse-
man,” are counted as one word.

Such names as O'Neill, MacDonnell, and
names with the prefix “8t.” are charged
for as single words, but double names liks
Bouverie-Tracy, although written with =
hyphen, count as two words. Initial lefters
are each counted as one word. e

Such abbreviations as “can’t,” ‘“‘won’t,”
“couldn’t’” are counted as single words.

Figures are counted at the rate of five
figures to a word. For example, '38563"
pounts as one word, while ‘385634 counts
as two words. Names of towns and vil-
lages in_the United Kingdom count as one
word. When words are underlined, or placed
in & parenthesis, or within inverted commas,
one extra word is charged for.

One word each .is also charged for the
.ordinary signs of punctuation if they are
required to be transmitted. s

When the address is beyond the limit of
free delivery, porterage is charged at the
rate of 3d per mile, or part of a mile, the
distance being calculated from the limit
of free delivery.

Telegrams (Forsign and Golonial).

Must be written on special forms and
ars accepted in the language of any of
the principal European countries. The
rates vary. Bee Official Postal Guide.

communicafion by Wireless Telegraphy
with Ships at Sea. >
Radio telegrams are accepted at an
Telegraph Office for transmission to ships
equipped with wireless telegraphic appara-
tus, through the coast stations at Boli
head, Caister-on-Sea, Crookhaven, Culler-
coats, Lizard, Malin head, Niton, North
Foreland, Rosslare, and Seaforth.
Further particulars of the sgervice, to-
gether with information as to charges,
can be obtained at any telegraph office.

COINAGE.

The British sovereign contains 113 grains
(Pure gold (worth 23th pence per grain)
should weigh 123.27 grains troy. The
term carat when applied to gold refers to
quality rather than weight. Gold can be
beaten into leaves one two hundredth thou-
sandth part of an inch in thickness.

One sovereign (1233 grains) can be made
to spread over 6,500 square feet.
